papers.title: TempCompass: Do Video LLMs Really Understand Videos?
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: ACL 2024 | diagnostic temporal benchmark
papers.thesis: TempCompass is the benchmark you read when you want to know whether a Video LLM is truly using temporal evidence instead of coasting on appearance and language priors.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns diagnostic temporal benchmark into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: The real problem is measurement validity: prior video benchmarks often blur temporal skills together and still leave static shortcuts available, so a model can look temporally capable without actually reasoning about order, change, or duration.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The core message is diagnostic rather than architectural: current benchmarks do not adequately measure temporal perception, so TempCompass creates a broader testbed and shows that existing models are weak on it.
papers.researchQuestion: It addresses the lack of benchmarks that separate different temporal aspects and task formats, so models can no longer hide behind static-frame bias or a single QA style.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The core message is diagnostic rather than architectural: current benchmarks do not adequately measure temporal perception, so TempCompass creates a broader testbed and shows that existing models are weak on it.
papers.methodSetup: TempCompass decomposes temporal understanding into multiple aspects, builds conflicting video pairs that preserve appearance while changing temporal structure, and evaluates with diverse instruction formats instead of one narrow QA template.
papers.coreInsight: The paper's deepest lesson is that temporal evaluation fails when appearance can stand in for time. A good benchmark must make temporal evidence indispensable, not merely present.
papers.readerValue: Read this first if you want a sharper standard for every later temporal paper: after TempCompass, you stop confusing broad video fluency with genuine temporal understanding.
papers.whyItMatters: TempCompass tells us whether a model is relying on static scene cues instead of temporal evidence, which is exactly the kind of hidden shortcut that often looks like temporal hallucination.

papers.title: MVBench: A Comprehensive Multi-modal Video Understanding Benchmark
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: CVPR 2024 | broad benchmark
papers.thesis: MVBench is the coverage benchmark: it asks whether a model that looks strong on isolated demos is still competent when video understanding is tested across many task types.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns broad benchmark into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: The paper argues that narrow evaluations overestimate ability. If a benchmark only probes one or two familiar formats, you learn very little about whether the model has robust video understanding.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The paper's main message is that existing evaluations are too narrow and that video models need broader testing to reveal their actual limits.
papers.researchQuestion: It tries to expose the gap between narrow benchmark success and real multi-modal video understanding across a wider task mix.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The paper's main message is that existing evaluations are too narrow and that video models need broader testing to reveal their actual limits.
papers.methodSetup: Instead of centering one temporal skill, MVBench assembles a broad suite of tasks spanning perception, action, causality, counting, order, and other video abilities so failure appears as a profile rather than one average score.
papers.coreInsight: MVBench teaches a useful benchmark-design habit: wide coverage is excellent for locating where a model breaks, but wide coverage alone does not guarantee that the hardest cases are truly temporal.
papers.readerValue: Use MVBench as your broad baseline. It tells you whether a later paper improves one narrow corner of video understanding or actually shifts the overall competence profile.
papers.whyItMatters: MVBench is important because temporal hallucination often looks less obvious when evaluation is too narrow; a broader benchmark makes shortcut behavior easier to spot.

papers.title: TimeChat: A Time-sensitive Multimodal Large Language Model for Long Video Understanding
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: CVPR 2024 | timestamp-aware video LLM
papers.thesis: TimeChat is best read as an argument that long-video QA needs explicit temporal anchors; otherwise the model can sound coherent while remaining weakly grounded in time.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns timestamp-aware video LLM into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: The paper targets a familiar long-video failure mode: the answer sounds reasonable, but the model cannot reliably tie that answer to the correct moment or interval in the video.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract frames timestamp awareness as necessary for long-video understanding and positions the model as time-sensitive rather than just language-capable.
papers.researchQuestion: It targets the mismatch between long-video understanding and weak timestamp sensitivity, where the model may summarize content without grounding it in time.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract frames timestamp awareness as necessary for long-video understanding and positions the model as time-sensitive rather than just language-capable.
papers.methodSetup: TimeChat injects timestamp-aware representations and supervision so the model reasons over video content together with explicit temporal positions, not just pooled clip semantics.
papers.coreInsight: If a model cannot say when with precision, it often does not really know what happened. Temporal grounding is part of understanding, not a cosmetic add-on.
papers.readerValue: Use TimeChat to train your eye for temporal faithfulness. It helps you separate answers that are merely plausible from answers that are actually anchored to the right segment.
papers.whyItMatters: If a model cannot bind answers to the right time, it may produce fluent but unsupported temporal claims, which is a common form of temporal hallucination.

papers.title: MovieChat: From Dense Token to Sparse Memory for Long Video Understanding
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: CVPR 2024 | memory design
papers.thesis: MovieChat is a memory paper at heart: it argues that long-video understanding fails early, at evidence retention, long before the language head gets a chance to reason.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns memory design into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: Dense token processing does not scale gracefully to movie-length inputs; important evidence gets diluted, forgotten, or made too expensive to revisit when the clip becomes long.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract frames sparse memory as a way to preserve useful video evidence while controlling the cost of long-context processing.
papers.researchQuestion: It tries to make long-video reasoning feasible by compressing dense evidence into a more usable memory representation.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract frames sparse memory as a way to preserve useful video evidence while controlling the cost of long-context processing.
papers.methodSetup: MovieChat compresses long video streams into sparse memory slots so the model can keep salient events available over long horizons without carrying every token forward.
papers.coreInsight: For long videos, memory is part of reasoning. If the model stores the wrong evidence or forgets the critical moment, later reasoning quality is capped from the start.
papers.readerValue: Use MovieChat to reason about long-video systems as pipelines. It gives you a concrete memory baseline before you compare retrieval-first or search-first methods.
papers.whyItMatters: Temporal hallucination often starts when the right moment is lost during compression, so memory design is part of the root cause.

papers.coreInsight: LV-Haystack makes the retrieval bottleneck impossible to ignore: in long-form video, evidence selection is often the decisive step and reasoning quality is downstream of that choice.

papers.thesis: This paper shows that a Video LLM can look correct on the first try and still be fundamentally unreliable if its temporal belief collapses under follow-up probing.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns temporal consistency into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: The paper argues that one-shot correctness is too weak a standard. A model that cannot answer follow-up temporal probes consistently does not really have a stable grounded belief about the event.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract argues that temporal comprehension should be measured by stable behavior across probes, not just by one-shot timestamp accuracy.
papers.researchQuestion: It studies prediction consistency as a key indicator of robustness and trustworthiness in temporal grounding, rather than treating a single correct timestamp as enough.

papers.methodSetup: The method starts from an initial grounding prediction, then probes the model with consistency checks to see whether its temporal belief survives paraphrase, verification, and contradiction pressure.
papers.coreInsight: Temporal faithfulness is not just about getting one timestamp right. It is about maintaining a stable, defensible belief when the same event is queried from multiple angles.
papers.readerValue: Use this paper when you want a sharper notion of trustworthiness. It teaches you to ask whether a model's temporal belief is stable, not merely plausible once.
papers.whyItMatters: Temporal hallucination is often not a single wrong answer but a lack of stable belief over time, and this paper makes that instability visible.

papers.title: MASH-VLM: Mitigating Action-Scene Hallucination in Video-LLMs through Disentangled Spatial-Temporal Representations
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: CVPR 2025 | hallucination mitigation
papers.thesis: MASH-VLM makes an architectural hallucination claim: action-scene hallucination arises because spatial and temporal signals are entangled too early, and the model must disentangle them to reason faithfully.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns hallucination mitigation into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: It targets cases where a Video LLM predicts an action from scene context or infers a scene from an observed action incorrectly.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract makes a compact causal claim: if spatial and temporal signals are entangled too early, the model is more likely to hallucinate action-scene associations.
papers.researchQuestion: It targets cases where a Video LLM predicts an action from scene context or infers a scene from an observed action incorrectly.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract makes a compact causal claim: if spatial and temporal signals are entangled too early, the model is more likely to hallucinate action-scene associations.
papers.methodSetup: The model combines DST-attention with Harmonic-RoPE so that spatial and temporal information stay more separated while still aligning with text.
papers.coreInsight: The paper sharpens an important point: many apparent reasoning failures are actually failures to represent order, transition, or duration in a stable way.
papers.readerValue: Use MASH-VLM as the main architecture-side mitigation paper for hallucination, especially when you want to compare benchmark-first diagnosis with model-side intervention.
papers.whyItMatters: It is useful because many temporal hallucinations are actually entanglement errors, where scene context leaks into event prediction or event order gets overridden by coarse spatial cues.

readingHighlights: The paper identifies two causes of action-scene hallucination: mixed spatial-temporal attention and standard RoPE bias.
DST-attention separates spatial and temporal tokens with masked attention.
Harmonic-RoPE changes positional handling so text, spatial, and temporal tokens stay better balanced.
The UNSCENE benchmark has 1,320 videos and 4,078 QA pairs.
The method achieves strong results on UNSCENE and also improves general video understanding benchmarks.

sourceSummary.one_line_takeaway: MASH-VLM argues that action-scene hallucination comes from entangled spatial-temporal features and positional bias, then attacks both directly.
sourceSummary.why_it_matters: It matters because many temporal hallucinations are entanglement errors, where scene context leaks into action prediction or overrides temporal cues.
sourceSummary.unresolved: The open question is whether the same disentangling strategy helps with longer multi-event temporal reasoning, not just action-scene association.
